title: Using homemade compost in containers and raised beds
subtitle: How to blend, when to use, and why straight homemade compost often needs help
excerpt: Homemade garden compost is excellent for improving beds but often too variable in nutrient content and drainage to use straight in containers. Mixed at the right ratio with perlite, grit, or bought peat-free compost, it becomes a cost-effective growing medium. The correct blend depends on what you are growing and how dry or wet the compost tends to be.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on making and using compost for containers (2024). Garden Organic growing media guide. Charles Dowding container growing notes. Soil Organic matter and plant growth (Rowell, D.L., 2014).

content.text: Straight homemade compost often has uneven texture: some batches are fine and crumbly, others lumpy and half-finished. Nutrient content varies by what went in. It can be dense when wet, which drains poorly in a container and can waterlog roots. Blending it extends its volume, improves drainage, and evens out variability.

Tomatoes, peppers, aubergines (pots and grow bags): 50% homemade compost, 30% peat-free bought compost, 20% perlite or coarse grit. This gives good drainage and a nutrient buffer from the bought compost.
Salads and herbs (window boxes, small pots): 40% homemade compost, 40% bought peat-free compost, 20% perlite. Quick-growing crops benefit from the bought compost's consistency.
Raised beds (permanent veg growing): 60 to 80% homemade compost, topped up each year with a 5 cm no-dig layer. Drainage is better in a raised bed than a container, so a higher compost ratio works.

Seed sowing: do not use straight homemade compost for seeds. Too much variation in fertility and texture leads to damping-off and uneven germination. Use bought peat-free seed compost for germination, then pot on into a blend.

Sieve homemade compost through a 10 mm mesh before using it in containers. Remove any undecomposed material, wood chips, or stone. The sieved fine material is suitable for mixing. The coarse material that does not pass through can go back on the heap for more time or be used as a mulch on beds.

Homemade compost provides nitrogen and some phosphorus, but its potassium content varies. Fruiting crops (tomatoes, courgettes, beans) need supplementary potassium from week four onward. Use comfrey liquid at 1:15 dilution every two weeks. Leafy crops (lettuce, chard, kale) typically need no extra feed if the compost ratio is above 50 percent.

Container compost used for one season is nutrient-depleted but still has good structure. Add 25 percent fresh compost (homemade or bought) and mix before refilling. Remove any root debris and check for vine weevil grubs (cream curved larvae, 1 cm long) before reusing. A container used for brassicas should not be refilled with brassicas: rotate to a different crop family.
